exercises.example_sentence: 雨が降れ__、ピクニックは中止です。
exercises.translation: If it rains, the picnic will be cancelled.
wrong_choice_explanations: たら indicates a conditional 'if/when' but emphasizes completed action — it doesn't fit here because we need the hypothetical ば form for this condition
なら indicates 'if' for assumptions about topics — it doesn't fit here because we need the verb-based conditional ば form after 降れ
と indicates automatic consequence 'whenever' — it doesn't fit here because we need the hypothetical ば form for this uncertain condition

exercises.example_sentence: 時間があれ__、映画を見に行きましょう。
exercises.translation: If we have time, let's go see a movie.
wrong_choice_explanations: たら indicates a conditional but emphasizes completed action — it doesn't fit here because we need the hypothetical ば form for this suggestion
て creates the te-form for connecting sequential actions — it doesn't fit here because we need the conditional ば form
なら indicates 'if' for assumptions — it doesn't fit here because we need the verb-based conditional ば form after あれ

exercises.example_sentence: もっと勉強すれ__、試験に合格できます。
exercises.translation: If you study more, you can pass the exam.
wrong_choice_explanations: たら indicates a conditional but emphasizes completed action — it doesn't fit here because we need the hypothetical ば form for this general advice
と indicates automatic consequence 'whenever' — it doesn't fit here because passing depends on many factors, not just studying more
から means 'because/from' showing reason — it doesn't fit here because we need the conditional ば form after すれ

exercises.example_sentence: 安けれ__、買いたいと思います。
exercises.translation: If it's cheap, I think I'd like to buy it.
wrong_choice_explanations: たら would need the form 安かったら — it doesn't fit here because we need the i-adjective ば form 安ければ
なら would need the form 安いなら — it doesn't fit here because we need the hypothetical ば form conjugated from the adjective stem
でも means 'but/even if' showing contrast — it doesn't fit here because we need the conditional ば form after 安けれ

exercises.example_sentence: お母さんが帰ってくれ__、夕飯を作ってもらいます。
exercises.translation: If my mother comes home, I'll have her make dinner.
wrong_choice_explanations: たら indicates a conditional but emphasizes completed action — it doesn't fit here because we need the hypothetical ば form for this polite request scenario
と indicates automatic consequence 'whenever' — it doesn't fit here because having dinner made depends on mother's willingness, not just her return
のに means 'despite/although' showing contrast — it doesn't fit here because we need the conditional ば form after くれ

exercises.example_sentence: 薬を飲め__、すぐに治るでしょう。
exercises.translation: If you take medicine, you'll probably get better soon.
wrong_choice_explanations: たら indicates a conditional but emphasizes completed action — it doesn't fit here because we need the hypothetical ば form for this general advice
と indicates automatic consequence 'whenever' — it doesn't fit here because recovery isn't guaranteed just from taking medicine
ながら means 'while doing simultaneously' — it doesn't fit here because we need the conditional ば form, not a simultaneous action marker

exercises.example_sentence: 毎日練習すれ__、上手になります。
exercises.translation: If you practice every day, you'll become good at it.
wrong_choice_explanations: たら indicates a conditional but emphasizes specific completion — it doesn't fit here because we need the hypothetical ば form for ongoing practice
なら indicates 'if' for assumptions about topics — it doesn't fit here because we need the verb-based conditional ば form after すれ
ために means 'for the purpose of' showing purpose — it doesn't fit here because we need the conditional ば form, not a purpose marker

exercises.example_sentence: お金があれ__、新しい車を買います。
exercises.translation: If I have money, I'll buy a new car.
wrong_choice_explanations: たら indicates a conditional but emphasizes obtaining money first — it doesn't fit here because we need the hypothetical ば form for this general condition
と indicates automatic consequence 'whenever' — it doesn't fit here because buying a car involves choice, not automatic action
けれど means 'but/however' showing contrast — it doesn't fit here because we need the conditional ば form after あれ

exercises.example_sentence: 天気が良けれ__、散歩しませんか。
exercises.translation: If the weather is good, shall we take a walk?
wrong_choice_explanations: たら would need the form 良かったら — it doesn't fit here because we need the i-adjective ば form 良ければ
なら would need the form 良いなら — it doesn't fit here because we need the hypothetical ば form conjugated from the adjective stem
のに means 'despite/although' showing contrast — it doesn't fit here because we need the conditional ば form after 良けれ

exercises.example_sentence: 日本語ができれ__、日本で働けます。
exercises.translation: If you can speak Japanese, you can work in Japan.
wrong_choice_explanations: たら indicates a conditional but emphasizes acquiring the ability first — it doesn't fit here because we need the hypothetical ば form for this general condition
と indicates automatic consequence 'whenever' — it doesn't fit here because working in Japan involves many factors beyond just language ability
から means 'because/from' showing reason — it doesn't fit here because we need the conditional ば form after できれ
